entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Last Christmas... I Wasn’t Exemplar! (GRC Christmas tune)
entry.content:
The year is 1984 (no, not in Orwell’s dystopian world) and so many good movies were showing at the cinema: Ghostbusters, Once Upon a Time in America, Dune, Gremlins, Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom... And The Terminator which seemed a lot lest close to reality than today with the advances of AI...

Going back to more joyful things, we blasted music from our Vinyl disks, including Wham!’s December release of it snow classic “Last Christmas” song.

What better occasion to commemorate the 40 years anniversary of its release, than to use it as a soundtrack for our famous – well, not nearly as much as those movies or the song but getting there – GRC Christmas tunes?

So, get your dancing shoes on, open YouTube if you need the beat since Vinyls are now long gone, put the sound up on your computer and let’s get this party started!

 

 

 

Background:

The day is Tuesday 17th of December 2024, and our singing hero is about to log off for the holidays and is reminiscing about her shortcomings during the year when it comes to controls checks, risk assessments, or following data protection guidelines.

 

Last Christmas, I thought I’d play smart,

Approve suppliers to pay, to keep them away,

Next year, to stay in the clear,

I’ll make the checks more substantial.

 

Next year, when audit re-starts,

I don’t want to stress out, and be ready to say (be ready to say),

My friends, there’s no need to fear,

I’ve reviewed invoices, it’s fine! (it’s fine)

 

Once wrong, and don’t deny,

How this could have turned, way more dire,

If there’d be a sanction’d party,

Well, I’m lucky it wasn’t.. but gee...

 

Happy Christmas!

Compliance wrote like a tweet.

With a note saying: we got you, and mean it!

Now with control monit’ring,

Detection patterns know-how,

You know you can now be Zen!

 

Last Christmas, I assessed with a dart,

On map where it’d stay... I know it’s cliché...

This year, I swear I’ll adhere,

Be ethic and act less casual.

 

This Christmas, I’ll be state of the art,

Won’t just do it midway, I promise I’ll play.

Next Year, I’ll be volunteer,

And act in all way exemplar (exemplar).

 

Oh... Well... Maybe!

 

Not doom’d, becoming more wise,

I won’t hide, or just roll the proverbial dice.

My colleagues will know I can be relied on.

Me? I guess it’s time I start the prevention.

 

It’s not just a cover, in organization chart,

I’m taking our security home to safe port!

Ooh, ooh, I need to rise above,

Won’t catch me off guard again.

 

Last Christmas, I let down my guard,

Shared my password most days, or left on display.

Next year it will disappear.

As this was far from ideal (ideal).

 

Last Christmas, didn’t mind the alerts,

Left mitigations to play, especially on Friday

When attacks breached our frontiers,

Lost things that were confidential.

 

‘coz there’re no buffer

Danger lurks from all the parts (from all the parts)!

No more looking over, it’s not for the faintheart.

For sure next year,

GRC will become one, will become a process vital,

Crucial!

The one!

 

I hope you enjoyed this last GRC Tuesdays post of the year. Thank you for following us once again in 2024, reading, sharing and commenting on these blogs and I sincerely hope you will continue to do so in 2025.

As usual, if you’d like to get in touch, feel free to add a note on this blog or send me a message on Twitter @TFrenehard

Finally, in case you’d like to continue singing GRC Christmas tunes, below are links to our previous beats:

  • 2023 – GRC Tuesdays: All I Want for Christmas is Cyber Safety
  • 2022 – GRC Tuesdays: Let me know! Let us go! Take a bow!, a GRC Christmas miracle
  • 2021 – GRC Tuesdays: The Famous GRC Christmas Carol version 2021
  • 2020 – GRC Tuesdays: We Wish You a Threat-Free Christmas and a Breachless New Year
  • 2019 – GRC Tuesdays: The Twelve Days of (GRC) Christmas

entry.title: Authorizations for SAP Business Technology Plattform - a quick start
entry.content:
1. Introduction to Clean Core:

For SAP customers, hybrid authorization concepts are especially vital under the SAP clean core strategy. With this approach the aim is to keep the core SAP systems lean, minimizing customizations for easier maintenance and updates: 

Having a closer look into the so-called side – by – side extensions, we recognize the following typical architecture: 

Business Data is transferred to SAP BTP (e.g. using SAP Smart Data Access – SAP SDA / SAP Smart Data Integration – SAP SDI) and stored in SAP HANA Cloud as part of a CAP Application running on SAP Business Technology Platform. 

Such an architecture requires protection on two different levels: 

1. Structuring the access to the apps via SAP Build Workzone Standard Edition Roles 

2. Restricting the access on business data level (e.g. display / change, restrict to dedicated company codes, plants, …) 

In this blogpost, we will focus on the second restriction level. 

2. Overview of SAP BTP Levels and their Authorization Management 

To understand the authorization management on SAP BTP, it is important to know the setup of the platform and its levels. When managing authorization on the levels, each is handled separately, and permissions are granted independently. For example, a role on a global account level will not be available in subaccounts. 

On each level below, predefined role collections can be granted. A role collection is a set of individual roles that includes permissions for various resources and services on SAP BTP. It can contain one or multiple roles. 

Global Account: 

The Global Account is the highest level in SAP BTP's organizational structure. 

It represents the contractual boundary of your SAP BTP subscription. On this level it is possible to create custom role collections besides the predefined role collections. 

Directory: 

Directories can optionally be used to group subaccounts for better organization and shared resource management. 

Subaccount: 

Subaccounts are created within the Global Account and are used to manage entitlements, quotas, and assign services specific to each subaccount. On this level it is also possible to create custom role collections besides the predefined role collections. The subaccount level is the most important level for authorization management, as you can create custom roles to protect business applications.  

Org (Organization): 

In the context of Cloud Foundry environments, an Org is used to manage and isolate resources. 

Space: 

Spaces are created within Orgs. It is a logical container that is used for app development, deployment and maintenance. 

3. Authorization Management on the Subaccount Level – Creating a custom role  

Creating custom roles for business applications at the subaccount level requires interaction across three domains. Here’s a step-by-step guide to help you understand this process: 

3.1 Provision Role Templates by SAP BTP Developer (grey box) 

The first step involves setting up role templates, which are provided by the SAP BTP Developer. These templates can be tailored within the customer’s business application based on existing attributes and scope. 

  • Scope: Defines the actions an app needs to execute specific functions, such as editing, viewing, or deleting. 
  • Attributes: Used to specify static or instance-based authorizations that vary depending on the context, such as location, department, or cost center. Attributes help refine business users' authorizations, particularly concerning organizational levels like plants or company codes. 

3.2 User Account and Authentication (UAA) Level managed by SAP BTP Authorization Administrator (blue box)

At this stage, role templates transform into roles and role collections. Here's how it works: 

  • A role directly corresponds to a created role template. 
  • A single role template can inform multiple roles by creating roles with different attributes. 
  • These roles need to be assigned to one or more role collections. A role collection can encompass multiple roles, enhancing flexibility in user permissions. 

3.3 Assigning Roles to Users by User Administrator (green box) 

  • Direct Assignment: Assign one or more role collections directly to users on the User Account and Authentication level. 
  • Group Assignment: Assign one or more role collections to user groups. Note that roles cannot be directly assigned to individual users or user groups. 

By following these steps, you can effectively create and manage custom roles for business applications, ensuring that users have the appropriate permissions to perform their tasks efficiently. 

4. Quick Start Service and Tool Support (by SAP Consulting)

The role concept for SAP Business Technology Platform can become complex quickly, if business data is stored as part of a CAP Application. Just let us validate the following (fictive) example: 

A medium / large size customer plans to cover his plant maintenance processes via a clean core approach and requires three extensions which are developed on SAP Business Technology Platform. All three applications have a simple authorization concept consisting of an admin role, an expert role and a display role, so we have overall 9 functional roles available. Decentralized access is required for 20 plants, so the security team has to manage 180 BTP roles. 

The SAP Service provides the following features: 

  • Knowledge Transfer Workshop for BTP Authorizations 

Learn how the Authorizations on SAP BTP are managed. Get a Deep Dive on the BTP Levels, Role Collections and Roles and Role Templates.  

  • A best practice basis role concept for a quick start of the SAP BTP development project 

The customer can utilize the best practice role templates provided by SAP to get a quick start on their project. This will help them quickly structure their role management for BTP operations, BTP security, BTP developer and tester. 

  • AIRC BTP Role Collection Generator 

- UI5 User Interface: 

The UI5 interface will provide the customer with an user-friendly way to manage and track their roles and role collections, making it easier for the authorization team to monitor and adjust access controls as needed. 

- Download Roles and Role Collections via Excel: 

The customer’s security team can use this feature to export their role and role collection configurations into Excel. This is beneficial for documentation, audits, and for backup purposes.

- Update Roles and Role Collections via Excel: 

With this feature, the customer will be able to make bulk updates to their roles and role collections through Excel. This is useful for managing changes efficiently, especially when adjustments need to be made across multiple roles or role collections.

- Transfer Roles and Role Collections via Excel across subaccounts: 

With this feature it is possible to transport roles and role collections across the system landscape (dev, qas, prd) as long there is no integration into transport management. Another use case is to transfer your basis role concept across different subaccounts.

If you are interested in this service, please get in contact with the authors of this blogpost.

5. Introduction to Authorization Management Service (AMS) 

Authorization Management Service (AMS) is a new, improved method to develop and manage user authorizations for SAP Business Technology Platform applications in a centralized way. This blogpost will not further focus on details of this service, but conclude with the general recommendations which technology to choose in which situation: 

  • For new developments, the usage of AMS is recommended 
  • It is not mandatory to update existing developments to AMS. However, using AMS can benefit your overall authorization management posture in BTP and help manage BTP authorizations more effectively. Unfortunately, there is no automatic migration to AMS possible so that developments must be adapted, and policies must be created and distributed “manually”. 

Currently, the Role Collection Generator just covers the classical approach. With the availability of corresponding interfaces, the role collection generator will also cover AMS. 

The following source provide some further information on AMS: 

  • Configuring Authorization Policies 
  • Assign Authorization Policies 
  • AMS in the CAP “Golden Path” documentation 

6. Wrapping it up – What are the key takeaways? 

Hybrid authorizations are crucial for SAP solution extensions in the context of the SAP clean core strategy. SAP BTP involves multiple levels where authorizations are managed separately. Custom roles and role collections can be created and assigned at the subaccount level, ensuring appropriate user permissions. Tools like the AIRC Role Collection Generator and Authorization Management Service streamline role management and enhance security. If you want to learn more about SAP S/4HANA authorization concepts, please visit our first blog.

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Threat Detection 2.0, SP07!
entry.content:
We are delighted to announce the release of SAP Enterprise Threat Detection 2.0 Support Package SP07 (Support Package Stack 51), which has been available to our customers since December 16, 2024. We are confident that this new support package will greatly enhance our customers’ security capabilities, providing peace of mind and robust defense against cyber threats.

SAP Enterprise Threat Detection remains the pioneering real-time security event management and monitoring solution specifically designed for SAP S/4HANA applications. It can be utilized both in the private cloud and on-premise. This advanced solution empowers companies using SAP software to swiftly identify and mitigate cyber-attacks in real time, preventing potential damage.

Our mission is to protect SAP S/4HANA applications and stop critical cyber-attacks, which are becoming more prevalent and targeting core business systems.

SAP Enterprise Threat Detection gives transparency into suspicious user behavior and anomalies in SAP business applications to identify and stop security breaches in real-time. Thereby, helping companies go beyond preventive measures and focus on real-time detection and response.

For those opting for the private cloud or on-prem solution, it can be delivered as a fully managed service by SAP or through our trusted partners. This state-of-the-art security monitoring service ensures customers don't need to invest in extensive internal security expertise or dedicated resources, allowing companies to focus on their core business operations while benefiting from top-tier security solutions.

Key Features and Business Value

The main features delivered with Support Package SP07 are:

Enhanced Alerts Filtering:

Security administrators can now find alerts more easily, filtering out non-critical alerts and focusing on the most relevant security threats. This leads to improved efficiency and faster response times, allowing security teams to concentrate on actionable items and reduce the noise from false positives.

Tightened Security Controls

Different users can have access to alerts based on their roles and responsibilities, ensuring that sensitive security information is accessible only to authorized personnel. This enhances the security posture of an organization by preventing unauthorized access to critical security data and ensuring compliance with internal and external regulations.

Transition to new ABAP Log Extractor Framework completed

(available since S/4HANA 2407, also read this article😞 Transitioning to the new framework allows customers to leverage the latest enhancements in log extraction, ensuring efficient and comprehensive monitoring.

Then improved log extraction capabilities enables a more robust and reliable security monitoring, leading to better detection and analysis of potential threats.

Support for New Logs Sources

Integration with additional log sources – SAP Analytics Cloud and Database table change logs – allows for a more holistic view of the security landscape and broader coverage on monitored activities.

  • SAP Analytics Cloud logs: Enhanced Security for Analytical Insights
  • Database table change logs: Comprehensive Monitoring of Critical Data Changes in Tables

This leads to a broader spectrum of monitored activities which improves the completeness and accuracy of threat detection.

New Patterns Added

The system is now capable of detecting additional security threats – mainly: alerts for unauthorized administrative actions and alerts for audit log tampering – which leads to enhanced overall security.

Additional Health Check Monitoring

The system can now monitor and alert administrators if there are any missing logs, which are crucial for forensic analysis and auditing. This ensures the integrity and completeness of security logs for more accurate threat detection and compliance purposes.

Automated Load-Based Refinement

with new configuration advisor report, to provide configuration recommendations and the ability to apply some of them automatically for log transfer from SAP NetWeaver AS for ABAP using ABAP Log Extraction (Legacy). This streamlines the configuration process, reducing administrative overhead and ensuring optimal performance of log extraction tasks.

Final Words

These enhanced features and capabilities provided with Support Package SP07, not only improve the detection and response mechanisms of SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, but also enhance the overall security and operational efficiency for our customers and service partners.

Where to find more?

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ENTERPRISE_THREAT_DETECTION/7a82a717136348359c705f5b34015d27/7710ab4a63f449fc9b2103574ecb63da.html

Cheers,

Arndt

entry.title: Navigating Compliance and Risk in SAP DevSecOps: What Business Leaders Need to Know
entry.content:
As businesses embark on digital transformation journeys, SAP solutions like SAP S/4HANA and RISE with SAP play an important role in streamlining operations and boosting agility. However, in this technological epoch, assuredly the first question rising to the fore is - how do you ensure security and compliance remain at the forefront of the transformation? The integration of DevSecOps in SAP environments offers a path forward. However, its implementation comes with its own set of challenges, particularly in compliance and risk management.

For business leaders, the knowledge to tackle these complexities will allow them not merely to safeguard organizational data but will add to their competitive advantage. The scope of this article will be to discuss the various challenges faced during SAP DevSecOps implementation and how organizations can mitigate security risks, ensure compliance, and maintain operational efficiency while driving innovation.

The Importance of DevSecOps in SAP Environments

DevSecOps is an amalgamation of development, security, and operations. It represents a shift towards embedding security as an integral part of the entire software development lifecycle which is why DevSecOps can no longer be an afterthought at the end of the development process. With the brisk pace of changes in SAP S/4HANA and RISE with SAP, security must be baked in from the beginning.

It is a business prerequisite for leaders to adopt DevSecOps in SAP environments. SAP S/4HANA often serves as the operational backbone of an enterprise, making it a hot target for cyberattacks and compliance risks. Accordingly, businesses must confront compliance and security head-on, ensuring that all components within the SAP ecosystem from cloud platforms to custom applications, meet stringent regulatory standards and move in a high-paced way as per the pains of businesses today.

The Key Challenges in SAP DevSecOps Implementation

While the benefits of a secure, compliant, and agile SAP environment are clear, implementing DevSecOps can be fraught with challenges. Below are some key obstacles businesses face when adopting DevSecOps for SAP.

1. Complexity in SAP Environments

SAP environments, particularly SAP S/4HANA and RISE with SAP, are mostly branched and tailored in different layers. As organizations build up their SAP landscape, they often tend to deploy a mixed bag of on-premises and cloud-based solutions. This hybrid setup can create complexity in securing every facet of the environment, which in turn creates a real challenge in implementing consistent security controls over all platforms.

With the delivery of more cloud-based solutions, ensuring that all cloud services represent SAP’s security and compliance requirements presents a constant challenge. The integration of third-party tools and cloud services into the SAP ecosystem may lead to gaps in security that could otherwise have been avoided, which will only increase the risk exposure.

2. Managing Compliance in a Dynamic Environment

Complying with regulatory authority is one of the most serious tribulations for companies operating in an SAP environment. For organizations using SAP S/4HANA, which houses sensitive financial and operational data, any possible non-compliance might burgeon to hefty fines, legal ramifications, and lifetimes' worth of irreversible reputational sag.

The dynamic nature of SAP implementations makes maintaining constant compliance difficult. As organizations innovate and customize their SAP solutions to meet unique business needs, new features and configurations must be regularly audited. However, ensuring these updates and changes don’t violate compliance requirements is a constant struggle. Additionally, the growing list of global regulations - such as GDPR in Europe or CCPA in California adds complexity to the process.

3. Tool Sprawl and Alert Fatigue

Many businesses encounter a phenomenon known as "tool sprawl," where they deploy a wide array of security tools to manage different aspects of their SAP ecosystem. While this can enhance security capabilities, it often results in an overwhelming number of alerts and reports. Security teams may experience alert fatigue, where the sheer volume of notifications leads to missed critical vulnerabilities or unnecessary focus on non-risk issues.

In an SAP environment, this challenge is particularly pronounced because of the many tools that need to be integrated to secure cloud services, databases, and custom applications. For business leaders, it’s important to establish clear prioritization methods for security alerts and ensure the tools selected integrate seamlessly into the SAP ecosystem.

4. Balancing Speed with Security

According to Gartner, “71% of CISOs say their DevOps stakeholders still view security as an impediment to speed-to-market.”

The demand for faster deployment in SAP environments, especially in projects like RISE with SAP, can lead to conflicts between security and speed. DevSecOps aims to streamline development while embedding security at every stage, but security checks often slow down the deployment process.

For SAP environments, where frequent updates and customizations are needed to keep up with business needs, maintaining a balance between speed and security can be difficult. Business leaders must ensure their development and operations teams don’t see security as a bottleneck but as an enabler of reliable, secure innovation.

Addressing the Challenges in SAP DevSecOps Implementation

Given the complexity of SAP environments, overcoming these challenges requires a structured and strategic approach to DevSecOps. Below are several ways businesses can address the key issues while implementing DevSecOps within SAP.

1. Establishing a Unified Security Framework

Developing a strong DevSecOps strategy involves the integration of security functions throughout the SAP ecosystem into a cohesive security framework. In this case, the framework would be needed to cover the cloud environments, applications, databases, and custom code of the SAP S/4HANA application. With the implementation of a holistic security strategy of this nature comes the opportunity to minimize the risk of unnoticed vulnerabilities and compliance breaches. At the same time, it promotes compliance from the beginning to the end of the software lifecycle.

Among the most important factors in this strategy is automating security testing within the CI/CD pipeline. Several tools, such as SAP Cloud ALM, promote visibility into possible security gaps so that teams can begin remediating such vulnerabilities early on, rather than waiting until deployment.

2. Emphasizing a Clean Core Strategy

For businesses implementing SAP S/4HANA, adhering to a clean core strategy can greatly reduce security risks. A clean core minimizes the customizations made to the core SAP system and pushes custom code and extensions to platforms like SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This approach not only reduces complexity but also ensures that updates and patches to the core system are seamless, ensuring that security and compliance aren’t compromised during upgrades.

By maintaining a clean core, businesses can more easily implement security measures that apply uniformly across their SAP landscape and reduce the time and resources spent managing complex customizations.

3. Cross-Functional Collaboration and Training

One of the most significant challenges in SAP DevSecOps implementation is overcoming the cultural barriers between development, security, and operations teams. Often, these teams work in silos, each focused on their specific priorities. To address this, businesses must foster a collaborative approach where security is integrated into the entire development and operations process.

Developers and operations teams must understand the security implications of their actions. Security professionals, likewise, must be well-versed in the intricacies of SAP systems and cloud environments. Bridging the knowledge gap between these groups will lead to more efficient risk management and compliance.

4. Continuous Monitoring and Real-Time Feedback

Continuous monitoring is integral to keeping the SAP environment free from threats and compliant with the desired security. Tools like SAP Cloud ALM offer businesses real-time insights into the system’s security posture that enable teams to identify issues and solve them beforehand. It is through a continuous feedback loop that businesses can proactively address vulnerabilities in their SAP environment and ensure that it remains secure, compliant, and agile.

5. Risk-Based Prioritization of Security Efforts

Not all vulnerabilities are equal, and businesses must adopt a risk-based approach to prioritize their security efforts. It is an approach that involves evaluating the severity of vulnerabilities in terms of their potential impact on the business. By focusing on high-risk areas first, organizations can reduce their exposure to major security threats while maintaining operational efficiency.

Looking Ahead: The Future of SAP DevSecOps

As SAP environments continue to evolve, so will the need for robust DevSecOps practices. In the future, businesses can expect more prominence of AI and machine learning in automating security testing, identifying vulnerabilities, and ensuring compliance. With increasingly complex and stringent regulatory compliance, the ability of organizations to adapt to new compliance requirements will easily emerge as a competitive advantage through an integrated security SAP implementation.

Moreover, the ongoing push for cloud adoption will require a more unified and agile approach to security, one that doesn’t go against the dynamism of innovation but pushes it forward, ensuring SAP systems remain resilient and compliant. The next wave of digital transformation will be led by businesses that master these DevSecOps challenges, turning their SAP environments into engines of secure and sustainable growth.

entry.title: How to use the new GRC Ruleset Manager (GRAC_RULE_SET_MANAGER Sample Excel File)
entry.content:
Hi Community of SAP,

In this article, you will explore the new Ruleset Manager in SAP Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) and learn how to effectively utilize the GRAC_RULE_SET_MANAGER sample Excel file. This comprehensive guide provides insights into configuring and managing rulesets. Whether you are an SAP administrator or a consultant, this article will equip you with the knowledge to enhance your GRC processes.

You can implement this program in sap with the links below:
3469294 Improvement note

3468630 Correction note

Thanks for your support for this process. Alican Atik (GRC Consultant)

  1. Download sample Excel Company_ARuleset.xml or create your own excel like this. (You can open with excel the xml file.)

    DOWNLOAD XML FILE (FROM GOOGLE DRIVE)
  2. Create sheets like included in sample excel file. (Or you can use for edit directly sample excel file.)

    1. Rule sets

      1. Insert Header/Headers RULE SET ID*
      2. Insert data below header whatever you want that named the rule.

    2. Rule set descriptions

      1. Insert Header/Headers RULE SET ID*, LANGUAGE*, DESCRIPTION*
      2. Insert data below header whatever you want.

    3. Risk-rule set relations

      1. Insert Header/Headers RISK ID*, RULESET ID*
      2. Insert data below header RISK ID* like XBS001 (Should start with X (It's optional but it's like common usage.)),
      3. Insert data below header RULESET ID* whatever you wanted Ruleset ID.

    4. Risks

      1. Insert Header/Headers RISK ID*, RISK LEVEL*, BUSINESS PROCESS ID*, RISK TYPE*, ACTIVE
      2. Insert data below header RISK ID*.
      3. Insert data below header RISK LEVEL* as GRACRISKLEVEL table. As you can see below customizing.
      4. Insert data below header BUSINESS PROCESS ID* as GRACBPROC table. As you can see below customizing.
      5. Insert data below header RISK TYPE*.
        You can use them as respectively:
        1- Segregation of Duties,
        2- Critical Action,
        3- Critical Permission.
      6. Insert data below header ACTIVE.
        1 is active. 0 is inactive.

    5. Risk descriptions

      1. Insert Header/Headers RISK ID*, LANGUAGE*, DESCRIPTION*, LONG DESCRIPTION, CONTROL OBJECTIVE
      2. I believe all data sections are clear for understanding. Only Control Objective side I do not know what about that. If you have any idea please write in the comment. Standart value is "-" without double quotations.

    6. Risk owners

      1. Insert Header/Headers RISK ID* RISK OWNER*
      2. I believe all data sections are clear for understanding.

    7. Risk-function relations

      1. Insert Header/Headers RISK ID* FUNCTION ID*
      2. Insert data below header RISK ID*.
      3. Insert data below header FUNCTION ID* like YBS001 (Should start with Y (It's optional but it's like common usage.))

    8. Functions

      1. Insert Header/Headers FUNCTION ID* BUSINESS PROCESS ID* SCOPE*
      2. FUNCTION ID* and BUSINESS PROCESS ID* is clear. SCOPE* side values is like:
        S- Single System
        C- Cross System

    9. Function descriptions

      1. Insert Header/Headers FUNCTION ID* LANGUAGE* DESCRIPTION*
      2. I believe all data sections are clear for understanding.

    10. Function actions

      1. Insert Header/Headers FUNCTION ID* CONNECTOR* ACTION* ACTIVE
      2. FUNCTION ID* is clear.
      3. CONNECTOR* As you can see below customizing.
      4. ACTION* is tcode. You can check on TSTC.
      5. ACTIVE values is 0 and 1.
        1 is active.
        0 is inactive.

    11. Function permissions

      1. Insert Header/Headers FUNCTION ID* CONNECTOR* ACTION* RESOURCEID* RESOURCEEXTN* FROMVAL TOVAL SEARCHTYPE* ACTIVE
      2. FUNCTION ID* is clear.
      3. CONNECTOR* as I explained above.
      4. ACTION* as I explained above.
      5. RESOURCEID* is authorization objects. You can check on TOBJ.
      6. FROMVAL* and TOVAL* is clear. It's values. You can check function SUSR_AUTF_GET_F4_HELP. If you want to fill single value, you can fill only FROMVAL*.
      7. SEARCHTYPE* values is AND or OR.
      8. ACTIVE* values 1 Active and 0 Inactive.

    12. Business processes

      1. As I explained above.

    13. Business process descriptions

      1. It's clear as I mentioned.
  3. Execute SE38 and write program name GRAC_RULE_SET_MANAGER and execute.
  4. Choose File Mode and Upload. Choose whatever you want append or overwrite. (I suggest Data Inconsistency Check.)
  5. If you choose Data Inconsistency Check you may encounter pop-up like below.
    You can check application logs on SLG1. Example is below.
  6. After everything you can click the Execute button and choose xml file.
  7. The program has no loading screen. If your file is large you need to wait. After finish you encounter pop-up like below.

    In conclusion, by utilizing the new Ruleset Manager in SAP Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC), you can effectively establish robust risk management and compliance processes. The sample Excel file and configuration steps provided in this article are customizable to meet the specific needs of your organization. Remember, you can always seek support from community for any challenges you may encounter during this process. The steps you take to enhance your GRC practices will significantly contribute to minimizing risks and ensuring compliance.

    Thank you for your potiental participation and support. If you have any questions or feedback, please feel free to share in the comments!

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What Risks to Look Out for in 2025
entry.content:
As it is now customary, I wanted to open the year with a first blog on the risk landscape and what experts, analysts, journalists, and business leaders raised as being top concerns for the year to come.

Since there have been quite a few changes in the ranking, I have also provided below a comparison against the last 2 years. You will also find the previous posts online:

  • GRC Tuesdays: What Risks to Look Out for in 2024
  • GRC Tuesdays: What Risks to Look Out for in 2023

Reminder of the state of Top Risk Categories from prior years:

 

2023

2024

 

If 2024 was a year of “risk consolidation” so to say, with a lot less fragmented and scattered responses and a clear consensus on Economic Conditions being the major concern for the year, things have shifted again and Information Technology is now the most often cited risk amongst all reports reviewed. It was already in the top 3 last year though, so this is not a great surprise. But there are a few explanations as to why this risk has risen, once again.

 

Top 5 risk categories in 2025

 

Consolidated top risk categories from 25+ recent publications

A few words about the methodology applied. As for previous years, I went over various risk reports (25+ and you will be able to find the details at the bottom of this post) and extracted the most often cited categories. These reports cover a range of industries and geographies, and the results are of course somewhat subjective as they rely on people’s opinions.

 

Information Technology

 

Information Technology rose from 3rd place last year to the 1st area of concern for 2025. Many reports cite recent major disruptions such as the CrowdStrike downtime to alert on what could be early warning indicators of a wider IT threat. As per the experts, this is mostly due to a concentration of services and technology among a few major providers posing the risk of a systemic failure. Kind of takes us back to the systemic risk that was on everyone’s mind during the great financial crisis of 2007... Thankfully, intervention and legislation prevented the full collapse of the system back then. Maybe as a parallel, some policy makers advocate for stronger regulation in this area as well. But the remedy here can’t be capital requirements as it was for the banking industry so they will need to find an adequate solution.

And of course, within this risk category is Artificial Intelligence. AI is reshaping all aspects of our lives and has been compared to a new industrial revolution – even wider in its reach. It could solve many issues... or create new ones. Once again, there are calls for more governance around AI to make sure that the genie is contained in the bottle or computer.

 

Supply Chain

 

If Supply Chain was of great concern during the Covid pandemic and, as a matter of fact, is systematically in the top 10 of all reports, it has reached 2nd position for 2025. The unknowns about potential trade policy restrictions from large economic powers (potential new tariffs from US towards Canada, China and Mexico, EU-Mercosur Trade Agreement reservations, etc.) account for a large part, but so does the increased sanction packages – especially from Switzerland, the European Union, the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia and of course the United States that “imposes three times as many sanctions as any other country or international body, targeting a third of all nations with some kind of financial penalty on people, properties or organizations” as per The Washington Post.

In addition to trade restrictions, the logistics aspect is another main driver of the risk level for this category. The fact that many hot spots for logistics – including for transport of goods, are located in disputed areas clearly introduces uncertainties. Some routes might have to be diverted if water levels in Panama Canal continue to decrease, if attacks on ships in the Red Sea persists... and trade routes in the Black Sea have already been affected with the ongoing conflict in the region.

Finally, a new aspect is also being flagged more frequently which is a direct result of above: a trend of retreating from globalization. This is leading to a strategic realignment with reshoring and even “friendshoring”. But I’ll get to that point in the very next category.

 

Geopolitics

 

Geopolitics is in the top 5 list, which is not a surprise since 2025 will once again be a year of political confrontation with key elections in major countries across all continents (Argentina, Australia, Cameroon, Canada, Chile, Czech Republic, Ecuador, Egypt, Iraq, Ireland, Niger, Norway, Philippines, Poland, Russia, Singapore, and Venezuela just to name a few) and also within major international organizations such as the UN Security Council for instance.

These elections could change the political landscape and even create new partnerships and alliances. Which brings me back to the “friendshoring” trend highlighted in supply chain. Here, instead of deciding to manufacture or source from most economically beneficial locations, the decision is instead to do so from countries that are geopolitical allies – hence pre-empting the return of trade wars or protectionism measures. Whereas multipolarism has been a source of stability for some time, these elections could also weaken political and economic unions. European fragmentation is always a key concern but so is the breakup of the G7 or G20 clubs. Especially if there were to be major political instability in one or more member countries.

 

Economic Conditions

 

With the rising cost of living, delayed interest rate lowering or even continued risk interest rates, and wage stagnation, consumer spending slowdown – particularly among younger generations, impacts Economic Conditions. As a matter of fact, economic forecast is not great for many established economies. Even if some countries such as the United States have shown a level of resiliency already. Global recession is a possibility and would lead to further liquidity and insolvency issues. Many of the factors presented above, especially the Geopolitical risks and the impact on manufacturing, will of course add further pressure here creating resource scarcity, cascading into increased prices for goods but also for materials and even energy. Return of inflation is therefore still high on Central Bank’s risk radar.

 

 

Cybersecurity

 

If Cybersecurity is in 5th position this year, just slightly below its previous rankings, it’s not because it’s going away. Simply that other risks are more top of mind in the various surveys.

In addition to operational disruptions and advanced frauds and attacks – especially AI-powered hacks, cybersecurity is also cited as a source of global distrust. One key concern raised by experts is the spread of disinformation that creates a lack of trust in public institutions. If this is true for local politics, it also impacts international organizations – weakening the likes of the World Trade Organization or the United Nations. Hence automatically increasing the likelihood of Geopolitical risks manifesting. It’s all connected really.

 

Positive Outlook

 

But it’s not all doom and gloom. Far from it.

The theme of the 2025 World Economic Forum is “Collaboration for the Intelligent Age”, so there is some hope that some of the above, including the trust factor amongst major stakeholders might be restored.

Closer to us, there is also the expectation that AI will revolutionize healthcare and enable early diagnosis of diseases for more timely treatment.

And, despite the underwhelming outcomes of the COP29, the Energy Revolution is in progress and the focus on climate change, biodiversity, and environmental sustainability is not just vain words. It’s an ethical, financial and regulatory reality for most companies. So there is great hope for 2025!

What about you, what risks do you and your organization have on the radar for the year to come? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments either on this blog or on Twitter @TFrenehard

 

Sources

 

If you’d like to drill-down further, I have shared below the sources that I used to put together the summary graph.

Of course, if there are other reports that you use and would recommend, I’d love to hear about them so don’t hesitate to add them in the comments section!

 

  • American Society for Public Administration (ASPA) – Top Risks on the Horizon for 2025
  • Aon – Global Risk Management Survey
  • BlackRock – Geopolitical Risk Dashboard
  • Capital Economics – Towards 2025: Risks to the global economic and market outlook
  • Control Risks – RiskMap 2025
  • Control Risks – Top risks for businesses in 2025
  • European Confederation of Institutes of Internal Auditors (ECIIA) – Risk in Focus 2025: Hot topics for internal auditors
  • Fitch – World of Worries: Political Risks in 2025
  • Forrester – Predictions 2025
  • Gartner – 2025 Risk–Based Audit Plan Hot Spots and Key Actions
  • Gartner – Top Emerging Risk Trends
  • Global Guardian – 2025 Global Risk Map Executive Summary and Key Takeaways
  • Grant Thornton – Banking 2025
  • Institute of Internal Auditors (IIA) – Risk in Focus 2025
  • Institute of Risk Management (IRM) – Risk Agenda 2025
  • International SOS – Risk Outlook 2025
  • Intuition – Top 5 operational risks for banks in 2025
  • KPMG – Top risks forecast
  • KPMG – Top Risks to Australian Business 2024–25
  • Maplecroft – The political risk ‘signals’ to watch in 2025
  • PKF Littlejohn – Insurance sector risks and priorities for 2025
  • PWC – Risk Management 2025 and Beyond
  • ResearchAndMarkets – Global Risk Heatmap Report 2024–2025
  • Supply Chain 24/7 – The top 3 risk trends for 2025
  • World Economic Forum – Three risks we aren't talking enough about

entry.title: Striking a Balance: Navigating the Open-Source Tightrope in a World of Evolving Threats - Part 1
entry.content:
By Philip Engelmartin, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

 

Understanding the Multifaceted Risks of Open-Source Reliance

Open-source software (OSS) is integral to the software development landscape, renowned for its perceived cost savings, innovation potential, and community collaboration. Nonetheless, heavy reliance on OSS introduces significant risks and complexities that organizations must thoughtfully navigate. This article delves into the numerous risks tied to OSS and underscores the necessity of addressing these challenges to build a more secure and resilient software supply chain.

The Open and Collaborative Nature of OSS: Both Asset and Liability

The open and collaborative nature of OSS development is a double-edged sword. While it drives rapid innovation and promotes community engagement, it also opens doors to vulnerabilities that malicious actors can exploit. These threats extend beyond simple coding errors and manifest in various insidious forms:

Malicious Contributors

One of the most significant risks is the potential for threat actors to disguise themselves as legitimate contributors and inject malicious code directly into OSS projects. Given the decentralized and often anonymous nature of OSS contributions, it becomes challenging to properly vet contributors. Consequently, the introduction and downstream propagation of malicious code become significant risks, compromising the security and integrity of software. The lack of formalized vetting processes in many OSS communities exacerbates this issue, making it easier for bad actors to slip under the radar.

Hidden Backdoors and Logic Bombs

Malicious code can be cunningly hidden within seemingly benign functionalities, activated by a remote command, a specific date, or an event. These concealed threats can leak sensitive data, disrupt operations, or provide unauthorized access to critical systems, potentially causing extensive damage before detection and remediation. The complexity and vast size of many OSS projects make it easier to hide such malicious code, increasing the risk that it will go unnoticed until it is too late.

Supply Chain Vulnerabilities

OSS projects often depend on a complex web of other open-source components, creating a vast attack surface. A single compromised component can trigger cascading effects throughout the ecosystem. Adding to this complexity is the issue of crypto agility - the ability to swiftly adapt to changes in cryptographic algorithms and implementations. Many OSS components rely on cryptography for security but may not be designed for easy upgrades or transitions, leaving software vulnerable [1]. The interconnectedness of OSS projects and their dependencies makes it difficult to track and manage vulnerabilities effectively.

Zero-Day Exploits and Public Disclosure

Vulnerabilities found in OSS are often disclosed publicly, giving malicious actors a window of opportunity to exploit these flaws before patches are developed and deployed. This race against time leaves organizations vulnerable to attacks that exploit these known weaknesses, highlighting the critical importance of prompt and effective vulnerability management [2]. Public disclosure also means that organizations must be ready to act quickly to mitigate risks, which can be challenging given the widespread use of OSS components.

The Log4Shell Incident: A Wake-Up Call

The Log4Shell vulnerability [3], discovered in the widely used Log4j logging library, serves as a stark reminder of the extensive impact a single OSS vulnerability can have. Affecting thousands of organizations globally, this incident underscores the urgent need for a more secure and controlled approach to software development. It emphasizes the necessity of proactive measures and robust security practices to protect against such widespread vulnerabilities that can devastate an organization's security posture.

Unpredictable Lifecycles and Support Gaps

The decentralized and often volunteer-driven nature of OSS development can lead to unpredictable project lifecycles, creating significant challenges for organizations that rely on these components:

Maintenance Gaps and Security Risks

Unsupported or abandoned OSS components are less likely to receive timely security updates, leaving software vulnerable to exploits. This lack of maintenance extends to cryptographic components as well. Without active development, OSS projects may fall behind in implementing the latest cryptographic standards or addressing newly discovered vulnerabilities in existing algorithms, exacerbating security risks. Organizations must invest significant resources in monitoring and maintaining these components to mitigate these risks.

Compatibility Issues and Integration Challenges

Combining OSS components with different versions and dependencies can lead to compatibility issues, requiring considerable engineering effort and potentially impacting software stability. These challenges necessitate rigorous testing and continuous integration practices to ensure seamless and stable software performance. Organizations must be prepared to dedicate substantial resources to managing these integration challenges, which can be particularly burdensome in complex software environments.

Lack of Service Level Agreements (SLAs)

OSS typically does not come with the formal support and SLAs provided by commercial software. This absence leaves organizations to rely on internal resources or community support, which can be inconsistent and unreliable, particularly when dealing with critical issues that demand immediate resolution. Organizations must often invest in building internal expertise to manage these support gaps, which can be a significant drain on resources and expertise.

Conclusion

While OSS offers many benefits, the risks associated with its use can be substantial and often underestimated. Organizations must carefully assess these risks and take proactive steps to mitigate them. Understanding the multifaceted risks is the first step in developing a comprehensive strategy to build a more secure and resilient software supply chain. This approach requires organizations to strike a delicate balance between leveraging the advantages of OSS and implementing robust security measures to protect against evolving threats.

Stay tuned for the next article in this series, where we explore the complexities of OSS licensing and geopolitical influences. We'll also be sharing how we're addressing the associated risks within our own use of open-source software in a later blog article.

[1] https://sweet32.info/  
[2] https://www.threatdown.com/blog/five-years-later-heartbleed-vulnerability-still-unpatched/  
[3] https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2021-44228

entry.title: Adapting to a New Reality: SAP’s Access Governance Solutions
entry.content:
With the rise of AI, cloud adoption, and stricter compliance demands, access governance—especially with fine-grained Segregation of Duties (SoD) analysis—has become essential. Industries such as finance, healthcare, and government face strict regulations (e.g., GDPR, SOX, HIPAA) that require safeguarding sensitive data and controlling user access. Access governance solutions are vital in ensuring compliance and security, reducing the risk of fraud, errors, and data breaches. 

Unauthorized access remains a primary cause of data breaches, leading to significant financial and reputational damage. A 2024 Ponemon/ IBM report highlighted that the average cost of a data breach is $4.45 million. By implementing SoD analysis, businesses can mitigate such risks by ensuring that critical tasks are distributed among multiple individuals, preventing potential fraud or mismanagement. 

SAP customers are in the process of moving from on-premises as well as implementing new cloud solutions to take advantage of the benefits they offer. Access governance solutions are required as critical in each of these transformations in all geographic areas, industries, and organizational sizes. To support these varied customer adoption challenges, SAP offers two key solutions for access governance: SAP Access Control (SAP AC) and SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (SAP Cloud IAG). These solutions cater to different business requirements, technology stacks, and implementation models. 

 

SAP Access Control: Flexible and Scalable for Large Enterprises  

SAP AC is ideal for large organizations with complex on-premises systems and hybrid environments. AC is available in both on-premise and private cloud deployment models. SAP Access Control has a combination of configuration options, APIs, and interfaces that enable extensive customization. The customization applies to each module and access governance process. Access Control has been implemented across SAP’s largest customers with broad application landscapes.

SAP Access Control Features 

  • Seamless Cloud Migration: SAP AC preserves custom configurations during cloud transitions, ensuring continued security and compliance. 
  • Fine-Grained SoD for Legacy & Modern Systems: SAP AC applies flexible SoD controls across both legacy and modern cloud-based systems. 
  • Compliance & Risk Management: It helps businesses manage SoD violations, ensuring adherence to regulations like SOX and GDPR. 
  • Flexible workflow engine – Based on SAP Business Workflow, this is highly flexible and customizable to handle different user provisioning and approval requirements. 
  • Extensive reporting – The solution offers a wide set out of the box reporting out of the box based on secured audit certifiable data.  
  • Emergency Access Management – Access Control includes a complete end-to-end solution for assigning temporary elevated permissions for urgent situations, complete with activity monitoring and post-session reviews.

 

SAP Cloud IAG: Designed for cloud - streamlined and optimized.  

SAP IAG is a cost-effective Saas solution that supports both on-premise and cloud applications with complete functionality and fast ROI. SAP Cloud IAG includes pre-configured templates, rulesets, and workflows with a simplified implementation process. IAG is built from the ground up on a multi-tenant Saas solution running on BTP. There is less to configure and once subscribed, customers get two tenants ready to run. All that’s necessary is to connect IAG with the business applications.  

There are two versions of SAP IAG – standard and integration edition. The standard edition supports full functionality while the integration edition is tailored as a cloud application extension for SAP Access Control. 

  • Faster Implementation: SAP Cloud IAG reduces deployment time with out-of-the-box access risk ruleset content and persona-based navigation. 
  • Scalability Across Hybrid Environments: It supports both cloud and on-premises environments, making it suitable for hybrid or multi-cloud strategies. 
  • Third-Party Integration: SAP Cloud IAG integrates with a broad range of third-party applications, ensuring consistent access governance across the entire IT landscape. 
  • Integrates/Extends Access Control – IAG, integration edition provides cloud system support for SAP Access Control. 
  • S4 Hana Public Cloud – The only access governance solution for SAP S4 Hana Public Cloud.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Seamless integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services for security and broad integration with SAP enterprise applications.                       
 

Conclusion: Securing the Digital Future with SAP Access Governance 

With SAP's comprehensive solutions, SAP Access Control and SAP Cloud IAG, organizations can ensure secure, compliant, and efficient management of user access across both legacy and cloud systems. Whether it's large enterprises with complex hybrid environments or businesses adopting cloud-first strategies, SAP provides the tools needed to protect sensitive data, reduce access risk, and maintain regulatory compliance. By leveraging the right access governance tools, businesses can safeguard their operations and build trust in an increasingly interconnected digital landscape. 

 

More information  

  • SAP Access Control Product Page and Documentation 
  • SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance Product Page and Documentation

entry.title: New attack detection patterns released for SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition
entry.content:
Seeing a pattern?

We're delighted to announce the latest delivery of 20 new patterns for SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition, extending our library to nearly 100 patterns and use cases!

These additions fall into four key areas:

  1. System-Specific Security
    - Focusing on ABAP, S/4HANA, and HANA systems
    - Includes critical debugging monitoring, system changelogs, and data protection
  2. Attack Detection and Prevention
    - Covering various Denial of Service (DoS) attack scenarios
    - Monitoring web request anomalies across different endpoints and users
  3. Access and Authentication Monitoring
    - Detecting unusual login behaviors and user activities
    - Identifying potential credential abuse or sharing
  4. Network and Communication Security
    - Addressing encryption standards and deprecated protocols
    - Ensuring secure communication channels

These new patterns aren't just rebuilding of existing content from our private cloud code line. Far from it!

The new versions have been adapted to the changing requirements in increasingly cloud-based system landscapes and evolving threat landscape; enhanced based on productive experience; and refined with a focus increasing the relevance of resulting alerts out of the box.

It's worth highlighting the inherent flexibility of all ETD patterns. Each pattern acts as a "super pattern," encompassing whole categories of suspicious or threatening cases. Thanks to our normalized and semantic data structure, along with the use of value lists, we can evaluate complex n:n(:n:n...) relationships. This means a single pattern applies to a wide variety of scenarios.

Moreover, we're excited to share another enhancement to ETDc further enhancing the patterns' flexibility: the ability to incorporate exceptions. This new feature allows for even greater adaptability, enabling you to fine-tune pattern behavior to your specific environment and needs.

Details and documentation in the help pages (logon required):  

https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5899f0627ef14f8db6fe04d0c5753ab3?locale=en-US

What do you think about this addition? Happy about your comments!

SAP Enterprise Threat Detection 

@Lingscheid @i353gfiata @eugen_pritzkau @michael_schmitt6 @KirtiSingh01

entry.title: Kickstart your SAP GRC Learning Journey: Introduction to SAP GRC, Submodules and useful links
entry.content:
1. GRC Principles & Harmonization - Introduction to GRC (GRC100) To begin your SAP GRC journey, you'll start with the fundamentals. GRC100 will give you an introduction to the principles of Governance, Risk, and Compliance. This foundational module lays the groundwork for understanding how GRC helps businesses manage risks, ensure compliance, and streamline governance. Also helps to understand basic harmonized reporting framework.

2. SAP Access Control (GRC300) SAP Access Control is a crucial element for managing user access within an organization. In GRC300, you will learn how to implement and configure SAP Access Control to ensure that users have the right level of access to the right systems and data, minimizing security risks.

SAP Access Control has submodules such as Access Risk Analysis (ARA), Emergency Access Management (EAM), Access Request Management (ARM), Business Role Management (BRM) and Certifications – User Access Review (UAR) etc.

Reference SAP Standard Learning journey:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/discovering-the-main-functionalities-of-sap-access-control

3. SAP Process Control (GRC330) SAP Process Control is designed to improve compliance with regulations and internal policies. It helps to implement Internal Controls. In GRC330, you will dive into implementing Process Control with all required configurations. Process Control is used to automate and monitor controls, making it easier to manage processes and ensure compliance across your organization.

SAP Process Control has submodules such as Continuous Control Monitoring (CCM), Assessments, Surveys, Ad Hoc Issue Management, Policy Management and Sign-off.

Reference SAP Standard Learning journey:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/discovering-the-key-functionalities-of-sap-process-control

4. SAP Risk Management (GRC340) SAP Risk Management helps to identify, analyze, and mitigate risks. This module is essential for those looking to build a comprehensive risk management framework within their organization.

SAP Risk Management has key features/sub modules such as Risk Planning, Risk Identification, Risk Analysis, Risk Response and Risk Monitor and Reporting.

Reference SAP Standard Learning journey:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/exploring-sap-risk-management

5. SAP Business Integrity Screening (GRC350) SAP Business Integrity Screening (BIS) is a new name for SAP Fraud Management (FM). It helps to detect and prevent fraud by screening business partners against blacklists, watchlists, and other data sources, and it uses CDS views.

SAP Business Integrity Screening (BIS) is one of the modules in SAP Assurance compliance Software (ACS), it has key features/submodules such as Detection of Suspicious Patterns (using Detection Methods, Detection Strategies), Predictive solution using ML, Fuzzy logics etc.

6. SAP Tax Compliance (GRC360) SAP Tax compliance is a critical aspect of governance, especially for global organizations. It helps you to learn how to implement SAP Tax Compliance solutions to ensure that your organization adheres to local and international tax laws, reducing the risk of non-compliance.

SAP Tax Compliance is one of the modules in SAP Assurance Compliance Software (ACS), it has key features/sub modules such as managing compliance management etc.

7. SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (GRC370) SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG) is a latest cloud solution for Access Governance. It offers similar features like On-Prem Access Control; however, it is still evolving. As most of the organizations shifting to the cloud, it’s essential to ensure secure identity and access management. It helps you control who has access to what in a cloud environment.

SAP Cloud IAG has submodules/services such as Access Analysis, Access Request, Access Certification, Role Design and Privileged Access Management.

8. SAP Audit Management (GRC380) SAP Audit Management helps to automate internal auditing procedures and improve quality.

It helps to setup end to end audit management and helps to plan, execute, and manage audits within your organization, ensuring that you're always prepared for internal and external scrutiny.

SAP Audit Management has key features/submodules such as Audit Planning, Audit Preparation, Audit Execution, Audit Reporting and Audit Follow-up.

I hope this helps to start your journey into SAP GRC also if you're aiming to level up your skills in SAP GRC ! By diving deep into these modules, you'll gain the knowledge and skills needed to excel in the world of Governance, Risk, and Compliance.

Please connect and follow me for the next upcoming informative articles in SAP Security, GRC, BTP, Internal Controls and Risk & Controls area.

Share your thoughts in the comments below !

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP solutions for International Trade Management, Enhancing Compliance
entry.content:
“New trade sanctions become operational” is a headline that one can regularly read these days.

For many companies – especially in the financial sector or sensitive industries, this has been a reality for a long time. But more and more organizations are realizing that these compliance requirements apply to them because they have dealings in geographies or with stakeholders that are now in the scope of a sanction regime.

Let’s roll back and start with the basics:

 

What are sanctions?

 

In short, sanctions are political and economic decisions resulting in restrictions placed on trade and financial activities. Failure to comply creates an offence that might lead to penalty measures.

And the list of activities that constitutes an offense can be quite long.

For instance, “trading, importing, exporting, selling, purchasing, transferring, transiting or transporting goods” of course, but also “providing brokering services, technical assistance or other services”, “making funds or economic resources available directly or indirectly to, or for the benefit of, a designated person, entity or body”, “failing to freeze funds or economic resources belonging to or owned, held or controlled by a designated person, entity or body”, “providing financial services or performing financial activities”… and more are detailed in the 2024 update to the EU Directive on the definition of criminal offences and penalties for the violation of Union restrictive measures.  

There are currently 14 ongoing sanctions regimes enforced by the UN Security Council, but many states – or economic unions, have their own set of sanction measures addressing specific sectors, territories or individuals. This is the case for the United States, the European Union, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, China, Canada, or Australia just to name a few.

Enforcing these ever-evolving measures is by no means an easy task, and keeping track of all the changes adds to the complexity. But breaking these sanctions is definitely not an option as, at the very least, it will lead to bad reputation, but also fines, loss of license to operate or even criminal charges can be enforced.

And, with predefined easy to use content from Mendel Verlag, this is precisely what SAP Watch List Screening has been designed to support as I am sure you already know.

But there are also cases where companies want to screen against custom watch lists: either coming from a new content provider, or maybe even internal restrictions, potentially conflicting parties, etc.

 

Introducing Custom Watch List Uploading

 

With the November 2024 update, Product Management and Engineering teams for SAP solutions for International Trade Management have introduced a powerful new feature: the ability to upload custom watch list content. This new capability enables organizations to enhance their screening processes proactively, safeguarding the company against potential risks arising from being involved with dubious stakeholders.

You can now seamlessly integrate your own watch list content into our SAP Watch List Screening solution using an API.

This functionality allows users to:

Personalize Screening Processes

Tailor the screening to your organization’s specific needs by adding entities that are particularly relevant to your operations. For example, this includes lists of fraudulent taxpayers, or persons and organizations with whom you prefer not to do business

Stay Up-to-Date

Regularly update your custom watch lists to reflect the latest information pertinent to your industry or region

Leverage Additional Data Provider Content

Include lists from different providers, or additional lists provided by Mendel Verlag which are not included in the sanctioned party list screening packages offered

The upload and management of custom watch lists are entirely under your charge, giving you full control over the content you screen against.

To achieve this, a new user-friendly Configuration app has been released:

 With this new Fiori app, users can:

Configure Watch List Package Categories

 

Assign appropriate categories to the content provided by Mendel Verlag, ensuring accurate organization and screening

Manage Screening Settings

 

Decide whether your uploaded custom watch list should be active and used during the screening process, giving you full control over compliance operations

Monitor Watch List Fill Levels

 

Keep track of the volume and status of your uploaded custom watch lists to ensure they are comprehensive and effective

 

Getting Started

 

If you are already using SAP Watch List Screening and would like to leverage this new functionality, simply follow the steps below:

 

 

 

Prepare Your Custom Watch List Content

Gather the data you wish to include in your custom watch list. This could be entities you prefer to avoid, or additional lists from data providers

 

 

Use the API for Upload

Utilize SAP’s API to upload your custom watch list content into the SAP Watch List Screening system

 

 

Configure with the New App

Access the new app to set up your watch list package categories, determine screening usage, and monitor fill levels

 

Find out more information

 

Should you be interested in reading more, you will find additional details at the following pages:

  • Feature in What's New in SAP Watch List Screening
  • Dedicated section in the Administration Guide for SAP Watch List Screening
  • REST API description in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Credits for the information in this blog go to Valentin Kruck and David Sommer, leading SAP Watch List Screening for Product Management and Engineering at SAP.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Beyond Prioritization: Combating Alert Fatigue Through Policy Enforcement at The Edge
entry.content:
By Jay Thoden van Velzen, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

Cybersecurity teams, developers and system operators increasingly struggle with alert fatigue. As new technologies, regulations and security tooling are rolled out, this comes with an increasing volume of security scans and events. The more investment is made in security programs, the more alerts these programs generate. Consequently, this can generate more remediation tasks than the organization can manage.

For illustration, research has shown that organizations have only the capacity to remediate 10% of the vulnerabilities in their environments within a given month. Teams are forced to prioritize what is most important to address.

 

The Security Alert Prioritization Funnel

To manage the volume of alerts, security engineering teams filter and enrich data, add contextualization, allowing us to prioritize alerts by criticality and urgency. They automate alerts where they can, to reduce the number of alerts to something that security- or other operations teams – whether Security Operations Center (SOC) analysts, operational or developer teams – can act on. This funneling takes a lot of effort, so it is here represented as a mountain to climb.

Initial filtering can be done on the raw security data sources of events. Not all logged events are necessarily relevant. Neither are all data elements of the logged event. Data enrichment with ownership and organization, and metadata associated with the resource where the event was raised contained in asset inventory management further contextualize the event with technical and business context. This allows for the prioritization of alerts based on where in the landscape architecture the event occurred, what the resource’s function is in the landscape and how critical to business operation. It also assigns which organization is responsible for the resource and who to contact to act on the alert.

Automated runbooks for autoremediation, response or containment, that is, Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR), can further lighten the load of the human operators at the very end of the funnel or buy them time.

Anton’s Alert Fatigue: The Study (2024) by Dr. Anton Chuvakin gives a good overview of this type of funneling within the context of security detection and response, and outlines challenges. But the same applies also to security configuration and vulnerability management, or alerts generated through security scans in the development cycle. All add to the alert volume that teams need to respond to.

 

Scaling Challenges

The problem with this model is that ultimately this scales poorly. Assuming that alerts are tuned well, with low false positives and appropriately assigned and prioritized, when the volume of the security events increases, this also increases the alert volume assigned to teams, and an increase in alert fatigue.

 Reasons for such increases are:

  • Growth in the size of the landscape and growth in deployed resources
  • Adoption of new technology platforms, such as public cloud, Kubernetes, or Software-as-a-Services (SaaS) solutions
  • Deployment of new security tools in areas not previously covered
  • Operationalization of new detection rules

To handle this increased volume of alerts at the end of the funnel, you can make the business case for more SOC analysts, developers or DevOps engineers. More likely you will be asked to do more with less. Automation, including with the help of AI, makes human operators more efficient and effective. But as the volume of security events increases, eventually you will be forced to an ever more brutal prioritization to get alerts back to a manageable volume that people can handle.

For illustration, let’s assume that we have 10,000,000 security events within a week that after prioritization and automation leads to 10,000 critical and high-value alerts, or a funnel reduced to 0.1% of the raw events processed. With 100 people to address those, that is 100 critical and high alerts per person per week.

What do organizations do when the events grow to 20,000,000, 50,000,000, or 100,000,000? Do they hire more people? Do they automate more? Can they afford to only address critical alerts, and accept the risk of deprioritizing the highs? Do they focus only on vulnerabilities listed in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog? How much can you prioritize before your security programs become security theater?

 

Policy Enforcement at the Edge

The enforcement of policies at the edge, that is, directly acting on resources that generate security events, can reduce both the volume of events to process, as well as reduce the latency between detection and remediating action.

Reduce Event Volume

If we can’t prioritize and optimize our way out of the problem, we must “cut” the volume of events going into the funnel – or at the very least, reduce their priority. Applying policies at the edge that guarantee secure configurations or ban certain behavior reduces the volume of events that must be processed, while still allowing for notification that the event has occurred but has been dealt with. That gives high-fidelity signals that something has been corrected but doesn’t require immediate action from anybody.

One such example of policy enforcement at the edge, or in the fabric of the platform, are SAP’s cloud guardrails. These guardrails enforce baseline secure configurations of cloud infrastructure when resources are created or modified and do much of the heavy lifting in SAP’s Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) program. Among other controls, these prevent publicly exposed Secure Shell (SSH) and Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections and allow such connections from known SAP IP ranges. That protects against operators unintentionally exposing such ports to attack but also stops malicious actors from exposing such services should they have gained a foothold in the environment in another way.

Cloud account owners are notified when these guardrails auto-remediate resources, allowing them to correct their deployment scripts, or raise an alarm when they find they didn’t make the change that triggered the action.

Other examples of edge or in-the-fabric policy enforcement are Admission Control in Kubernetes clusters, enforced image scans and build checks. While those are primarily to ensure secure software development and deployment, they also ensure once deployed that adversaries can’t, for instance:

  • Deploy overprivileged containers
  • Deploy containers that don’t come from approved image registries
  • Deploy containers that lack required code signing

That adds defense-in-depth barriers that add obstacles to potential attackers, increases cyber resilience, and buys security operations time to detect and respond.

Reduce Latency

When human operators need to intervene, the time to resolve an issue can be very long. Research from Bitsight indicates that organizations in 2023 took nearly 4.5 months to remediated critical severity KEV vulnerabilities, and more than 9 months for high severity. That is in line with research from Mend.io that found that on average it takes 271 days to fix a vulnerability. Well-resourced organizations may be able to reduce that to weeks, but that still leaves a window for exploitation.

Response to suspected detected security incidents will be much faster. But in high-velocity landscapes, resources may only exist for hours, minutes or seconds, and be destroyed by the time a human operator can react.

 Automation through the funnel pipeline has a latency of its own, just to get alerts into the hands of the right people or to drive an automated response. The collection and ingestion of events, data enrichment and contextualization, and prioritization of alerts all require data collection, pipelining and processing. The higher the event volume, the more computational time that takes. Your SOAR automation may not be fast enough if it has to wait for that processing to complete.

Policy enforcement at the edge, on the host or in the cloud fabric, and before going through this event data pipeline reduces response latency.

 

Challenges and More Sophisticated Controls

Edge policies are blunt instruments. They operate before adding organizational context, and without the nuance that that enables. To take the public SSH and RDP example again, that guardrail applies to any cloud account and workload, whether running customer solutions, internal use by development, or a team running a sandbox account for experiments. Edge policies only work where their impact doesn’t create an operational problem for legitimate use cases or cause outages. Exceptions for edge cases can be complex to implement. The more varied and complex your environment, the harder it may be to implement a broad policy that enforces a particular configuration or bans certain behavior from the landscape.

The rollout of edge policies also requires more care. The cloud guardrails are deployed in four “deployment rings” where over the course of a quarter an increasing number of cloud accounts are onboarded, to minimize unforeseen disruptions. By first deploying to test and pre-production accounts, issues are bound to emerge during testing before they impact production environments, and the policy can be corrected or even withdrawn from the rollout.

Other controls can be too risky to implement. Forced autorotation of encryption keys or downgrade of authorizations for a seemingly overprivileged admin or service account can have devastating downstream impact. Auto-updates of vulnerable software components can have unpredictable consequences when not managed through appropriate development and IT operations processes. It isn’t possible to eliminate the funnel altogether.

However, based on its effectiveness in CSPM, SAP is exploring if such edge controls can be taken further. Aside from autoremediating misconfigurations and deployment controls, we are researching whether certain behaviors can be banned from the landscape. Technologies like eBPF allow us to reconsider what is possible. This is primarily used for observability through its direct visibility into the Linux kernel, including system calls and network events, for both IT operations and security use cases.

But eBPF also allows for the interception of system calls and network events, and therefore the opportunity to apply policies to block them at runtime. This could potentially ban reverse shells from the landscape, by blocking the setup of network sockets to a remote address with an interactive shell.
